summary: Well-structured product ideal for fleet monitoring. Thorough policy decomposition via .means() blocks gives each design constraint a clear rationale. Causal chains from worker ticks through collection, storage, and dashboard rendering are complete and recoverable without external context. Minor issues: a bare comment violates Agentish norms, the UI section is thinner than collection/storage, and some enforces rules are restated in policy means blocks.
strengths: Excellent policy decomposition — seven named policies each with concrete .means() blocks that preload rationale and constrain implementation without dictating it
Complete data flow topology from worker-side collection through manager storage to dashboard rendering, all recoverable from the file alone
Strong causal chains: triggers map cleanly to collection actions, policies, and storage effects with no dangling or ambiguous paths
Precise definitions block reduces interpretive slack on key terms like SparseProcessSampling, NoBurstOnlyCapture, and ProcessRingBuffer
Abstraction stays consistently at product-ideal level — names concrete fields (pid, comm, cpuPercent, rssBytes) without dropping into implementation code
weaknesses: Line 3 bare comment '// Swarm Monitor' violates Agentish rejection of comments; the role attribute on SwarmMonitor already carries this information
UI/dashboard section is notably thinner than collection and storage sections — mostly enumerates screen contents without behavioral rules or priority constraints
Some redundancy between the enforces block and individual policy .means() blocks (e.g., NoBurstOnlyCapture restates enforces rule 3 almost verbatim)
No explicit retention or TTL policy for stored metrics — a product-ideal gap given the emphasis on bounded payloads and crash lead-up windows
